comments.text: This paper claims a few interesting results on this problem, but no full solution.

GPT Pro suggests that this should be deducible fairly quickly from standard results in sieve theory. The key point is to prove that $\lvert A\cap [n-x,n)\rvert\leq \pi(x)+O(x/(\log x)^2)$ for all $x$, from which this follows by partial summation. The contribution from those $a\in A\cap [n-x,n)$ with a prime factor $<x$ is at most $\pi(x)$, since any prime $p\leq x$ can divide at most one such $a$. So it suffices to bound the number of $a$ which are $x$-rough, which it claims is $\ll x/(\log x)^2$. I haven't checked the details, so it may be being a little optimistic here, but it seems to perform such arguments well, so presumably this works out; I'll let others with more experience confirm this (or, even better, formalise this!) EDIT: Actually I am a little sceptical now, since $A$ may contain many primes in $[n-x,n)$, and it seems difficult to show there is not a large contribution from such primes. (cf. [855]).

I also believe this doesn't work. That estimate would imply $\pi(x+y) \le \pi(x) + \pi(y) + O(y/(\log y)^2)$, which is one of the conjectures in the description of [855]. So it doesn't seem like standard results can settle this. Credit to GPT-5.4 Thinking for discussion.
